tips.memoryTip: Picture development as a theatre: most animal-cell roles are cast in the opening scene, while many plant cells keep auditions open for life.
tips.groundingRationale: The excerpt contrasts most animal cells differentiating early with many plant cells retaining that ability throughout life; the theatre is an explicit mnemonic.

tips.memoryTip: Picture a differentiating cell receiving a job toolkit: the tools stand for different sub-cellular structures that enable its particular function.
tips.groundingRationale: The excerpt states that differentiation equips a cell with different sub-cellular structures enabling it to perform a certain function.

tips.memoryTip: Memory image: plant and animal genetic material is locked in a safe labelled NUCLEUS; bacterial genetic material has no such enclosure.
tips.groundingRationale: The excerpt says plant and animal genetic material is enclosed in a nucleus, whereas bacterial genetic material is not; the safe is mnemonic imagery.

tips.memoryTip: Picture a mature animal’s cell-division office with its main work filed in two matching R drawers: REPAIR and REPLACEMENT.
tips.groundingRationale: The excerpt states that cell division in mature animals is mainly restricted to repair and replacement; the two R drawers provide a sound and spatial cue.

tips.memoryTip: Memory image: VACUOLE sounds like a vacuum flask; picture that permanent plant-cell flask with a contents label: CELL SAP.
tips.groundingRationale: The excerpt explicitly identifies a permanent plant-cell vacuole filled with cell sap; the vacuum-flask resemblance is labelled as a memory image.

tips.memoryTip: Picture the alpha-scattering conclusion as a target: the atom’s MASS sits on the central bullseye labelled NUCLEUS, rather than spread across it.
tips.groundingRationale: The excerpt states that alpha scattering showed atomic mass concentrated at the centre, the nucleus; the target supplies the spatial retrieval cue.

tips.memoryTip: Picture zooming into an ELEMENT until a red stop sign appears: ATOM. The sign marks the smallest part of that element that can exist.
tips.groundingRationale: The excerpt defines an atom as the smallest part of an element that can exist; the zoom and stop sign encode that endpoint.

tips.memoryTip: Picture Bohr’s model as a dartboard: nucleus at the bullseye, electrons circling on marked rings; each ring cues a specific distance.
tips.groundingRationale: The excerpt places the nucleus at the centre and states that Bohr proposed electrons orbiting it at specific distances; the dartboard is mnemonic imagery.

tips.memoryTip: Picture a compound as a padlocked recipe: its elements are chemically joined, and the ingredient proportions cannot slide—they stay fixed.
tips.groundingRationale: The excerpt defines compounds as elements chemically combined in fixed proportions; the locked recipe links both defining features.

tips.memoryTip: Picture a MASS NUMBER guest list with only two particle types admitted: PROTONS + NEUTRONS; ELECTRONS are crossed off.
tips.groundingRationale: The excerpt defines mass number as the sum of protons and neutrons, supporting their inclusion and the exclusion of electrons.

tips.memoryTip: Picture a mixture as a shared room, not a chemical marriage: its substances do not chemically join, and each keeps the same chemical-properties badge.
tips.groundingRationale: The excerpt states that mixture substances are not chemically combined and that each substance’s chemical properties remain unchanged.

tips.memoryTip: Picture an atom’s charge ledger: every electron is matched by a proton; equal totals make the final line read NO OVERALL CHARGE.
tips.groundingRationale: The excerpt directly links equal electron and proton numbers in an atom with having no overall electrical charge.

tips.memoryTip: Picture a pudding-shaped ball stamped +, with tiny − electron dots embedded like fruit: the ball is positive; the embedded electrons are negative.
tips.groundingRationale: The excerpt describes a ball of positive charge containing embedded negative electrons; the dessert layout makes that charge arrangement vivid.

tips.memoryTip: Picture a red gate marked LIMIT OF PROPORTIONALITY: the elastic-energy equation carries a pass valid only while that gate has not been crossed.
tips.groundingRationale: The excerpt conditions the elastic potential energy equation on the limit of proportionality not having been exceeded; the gate encodes that boundary.
